title: Why Are Gas Prices Going Up?
summary: Gas prices are going up in the UK because of a war in the Middle East. This war started in late February and has made it harder to get oil, which is used to make petrol and diesel for cars. When oil is harder to get, it costs more, and that makes filling up your car more expensive. Since the war began, oil prices have jumped a lot, from $73 to $106 per barrel. Experts say that for every $10 increase in oil, gas prices go up by about 7p per litre. Right now, petrol costs around 137.51p per litre and diesel is about 150.97p. People think prices will keep rising, with petrol maybe hitting 140p soon and diesel going to 160p. The UK buys most of its oil from other countries like the US and Norway, so when global prices go up, we pay more too.

questions.correct_answer: From other countries like the US and Norway

background_read: Gas prices are the money you pay to put fuel in a car. Oil is a natural resource found underground and used to make many things, including petrol and diesel. Wars can affect how much oil is available because they might damage places where oil is produced or transported. The UK imports oil, meaning it buys it from other countries, so when there's a problem elsewhere, it can make things more expensive here. Understanding this helps explain why filling up a car costs more sometimes.
Article_Structure: WHO: Motorists in the UK and experts like Simon Williams from the RAC
WHAT: Gas prices are rising due to higher oil costs from a war
WHERE: In the UK and the Middle East where the war is happening
WHY: Because the war disrupts oil supply, making it more expensive and increasing fuel prices

perspectives.description: Higher fuel prices make driving more costly for people and can affect other goods too
